cards.text: A {{c1::constructor}} is a special member function that is automatically called when an object of the class is {{c2::instantiated}}.
Constructors have the {{c1::same name}} as the class, have {{c2::no return type}} (not even void), and are typically {{c3::public}}.

cards.question: How do you define a constructor?
cards.explanation: Constructor has same name as class, no return type, and initializes the members.

cards.back: Constructors are called automatically when an object is created. You don't explicitly call constructors - they're invoked during object instantiation.

cards.text:
To instantiate an object with constructor parameters:
{{c1::Fraction f{3, 4};}}  // using list initialization
{{c2::Fraction f(3, 4);}}  // using direct initialization

cards.question: What's wrong with this constructor?
cards.answer: This is NOT a constructor - it has a return type (void). Constructors must have no return type at all.
cards.explanation: This is actually a regular member function named Foo that returns void. Constructors have no return type, not even void.

cards.concept: Why use constructors instead of default member initializers
cards.explanation:
Constructors allow:
  • Accepting parameters to initialize members with different values
  • Performing validation or calculations during initialization
  • Initializing members that depend on parameters
  • Enforcing that certain values must be provided at creation time
Default member initializers only work for fixed default values.
examples: Fraction(int n, int d) { m_num = n; m_den = d; }  // flexible
int m_value{0};  // only for fixed default

cards.front: Can a class have multiple constructors?
cards.back: Yes! A class can have multiple constructors with different parameters (constructor overloading). The compiler chooses which one to call based on the arguments provided.

cards.text: Constructors are typically made {{c1::public}} so objects can be created from outside the class. Making them {{c2::private}} prevents object creation from outside the class.

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.code:
class Point
{
public:
    Point(int x, int y)
    {
        std::cout << "Point(" << x << ", " << y << ") constructed";
    }
};

Point p{3, 4};
cards.explanation: The constructor is called automatically when p is created, executing the code inside the constructor body.

cards.front: Can you call a constructor explicitly after an object is created?
cards.back: No. Constructors are only called during object creation. After an object exists, you cannot call its constructor again. If you need to reinitialize, you must create a new object or use a setter function.

cards.text: Best practice: Constructors should {{c1::initialize}} all member variables to ensure objects start in a {{c2::valid state}}.
